proposal.reasoning: The optimization is at a plateau (stuck_counter=5) with conflicting property trends: density and heat of vaporization are underestimated (indicating weak van der Waals cohesion) while surface tension is significantly overestimated (indicating excessive electrostatic contribution). The current best parameters (charges ±0.26, dummy bonds 1.2Å) produce strong electrostatics at the interface but insufficient bulk cohesion. To break this plateau, I propose a decoupling strategy: (1) Reduce dummy atom charges toward the lower bound (0.235-0.25) to decrease electrostatic contribution to surface tension, (2) Compensate by maximizing dummy bond lengths to 1.22-1.23Å (near upper bound) to maintain the target dipole moment via geometric separation (μ = q × r), (3) Strengthen Lennard-Jones attractions by increasing |MS2_OCB_epsilon| from -0.25 to -0.30/-0.35 to boost density and ΔH_vap, and (4) Reduce equilibrium angles toward 133-140° to increase molecular flexibility and disrupt ordered interfacial packing, further lowering surface tension. Four diverse forks explore this trade-off space: Fork 1 applies conservative charge reduction (0.25) with moderate epsilon increase; Fork 2 aggressively reduces charges (0.24) with strong vdW (-0.35) and significant angle bending; Fork 3 tests maximum angle reduction (133°) with intermediate parameters; Fork 4 explores a slightly reduced rmin (5.15) to test packing optimization while keeping other changes moderate.

hypothesis.scientific_rationale: Reducing partial charges on dummy atoms (from 0.26 to 0.235-0.25) decreases the electrostatic contribution to surface tension, which is currently overestimated by 12-15%. Simultaneously increasing dummy bond lengths to their maximum allowed values (1.22-1.23Å) maintains the molecular dipole moment (μ = q × d) despite lower charges, addressing the previously observed dipole underestimation. Strengthening the Lennard-Jones epsilon parameter (from -0.25 to -0.30 to -0.35) enhances van der Waals cohesive energy to correct the underestimated density (2-3% low) and heat of vaporization (10-11% low). Finally, reducing equilibrium angles (from 145° toward 133-140°) increases molecular bending flexibility, disrupting ordered interfacial packing geometry and reducing surface tension through entropic effects at the interface.
hypothesis.expected_benefit: This combined approach will simultaneously increase bulk density and heat of vaporization through stronger vdW attractions while reducing surface tension through weakened electrostatics and less ordered interfacial geometry. The dipole moment will remain near target (3.96 D) due to the compensatory increase in charge separation distance. This decoupling resolves the current conflict where high surface tension coexists with low density/HVap.
hypothesis.test_method: The hypothesis will be tested by evaluating whether the four parameter sets produce: (1) Surface tension reduced toward 42-44 dyne/cm (from current ~48.5), (2) Density increased toward 1.08-1.09 g/cm³, (3) HVap increased toward 12.0-12.4 kcal/mol, and (4) Dipole moment maintained at 3.9-4.0 D, while avoiding simulation instabilities.

hypothesis.scientific_rationale: Increasing the magnitude of LJ epsilon parameters (making them more negative from -0.25/-0.35 toward -0.30 to -0.48) strengthens van der Waals cohesive interactions, which will increase bulk density and heat of vaporization toward target values. Simultaneously, decreasing dummy partial charges (from ±0.26 to ±0.20-0.23) and reducing dummy bond lengths (from 1.2 Å to 1.0-1.15 Å) decreases the molecular dipole moment, reducing excessive dipole-induced surface ordering that causes overestimated surface tension at 298K.
hypothesis.expected_benefit: This dual-parameter adjustment will simultaneously correct the low density (4.8% error) and low HVap (6.9% error) by strengthening bulk cohesion, while reducing surface tension (10.1% high) by moderating interfacial dipole alignment. The temperature-dependent surface tension behavior (large error at 298K, small at 323K) should improve as the reduced dipole weakens temperature-sensitive orientational ordering at the interface.
hypothesis.test_method: The hypothesis will be tested by evaluating whether the four parameter sets produce: (1) Density values approaching 1.095 g/cm³ at 298K, (2) HVap values approaching 12.6 kcal/mol, (3) Surface tension reduced toward 42 mN/m at 298K with maintained accuracy at 323K, and (4) Dipole moments closer to the target 3.96 D.
